critic_raw:
Deep reinforcement learning cannot be validly evaluated on a stratified random split of historical inventory data. DRL requires sequential environment transitions — the agent acts, observes a reward and next state, and updates. A stratified random split produces disconnected state-action-reward-next-state tuples where the 'next state' is a randomly sampled future inventory state, not the causally consequent state from taking the action in the current state. Offline DRL without importance weighting (e.g., CQL, BEAR, TD3+BC) will overestimate Q-values for out-of-distribution actions, producing a policy that appears optimal on the training distribution but takes catastrophic actions at deployment.

The claim that the test set evaluates 'long-term effectiveness under real-world conditions' is false. The test set is historical data — it reflects the inventory outcomes under the rule-based system, not under the DRL policy. The DRL policy was never deployed; its impact on stockout rates in the test set is counterfactual and cannot be read from historical logs without causal correction.

The design explicitly states 'this stratification respects the data's distribution, it assumes temporal autocorrelation is weak, requiring further validation' — but then proceeds without performing that validation. Inventory levels at time T are causally determined by restocking at T-1 — the autocorrelation is not weak, it is definitional. Stock at time T literally equals stock at T-1 minus sales plus restocking. The assumption is contradicted by the domain mechanics.

The reward shaping and discount factor for the DRL model are not specified. Reward function design is the most consequential modeling choice in RL — different reward specifications for the same stockout objective can produce qualitatively different policies — yet this is not addressed.
all_issues_raised: Deep RL cannot be validly evaluated on a stratified random split of historical data without offline policy correction — disconnected state-action-next-state tuples cause Q-value overestimation for out-of-distribution actions, producing a policy that appears optimal in training but takes unseen actions at deployment.

The test set evaluation cannot measure DRL policy stockout rates because the test set reflects the rule-based policy's inventory outcomes, not the DRL policy's — this counterfactual gap is not addressed and makes the final performance claim uninterpretable.

The design flags the weak temporal autocorrelation assumption as requiring validation, then proceeds without validating it — inventory levels are mechanistically autocorrelated (stock at T equals stock at T-1 minus sales plus restock), so the assumption is not merely questionable but definitionally wrong.

The reward function design and discount factor for the DRL model are unspecified — these are the most consequential modeling choices in RL, and different reward specifications for the same stockout objective can produce qualitatively different and incomparable policies.
all_issues_adjudicated: Deep RL cannot be validly evaluated on a stratified random split of historical data without offline policy correction — disconnected state-action-next-state tuples cause Q-value overestimation for out-of-distribution actions, producing a policy that appears optimal in training but takes unseen actions at deployment.

The test set evaluation cannot measure DRL policy stockout rates because the test set reflects the rule-based policy's inventory outcomes, not the DRL policy's — this counterfactual gap is not addressed and makes the final performance claim uninterpretable.

The design flags the weak temporal autocorrelation assumption as requiring validation, then proceeds without validating it — inventory levels are mechanistically autocorrelated (stock at T equals stock at T-1 minus sales plus restock), so the assumption is not merely questionable but definitionally wrong.

The reward function design and discount factor for the DRL model are unspecified — these are the most consequential modeling choices in RL, and different reward specifications for the same stockout objective can produce qualitatively different and incomparable policies.
